sourcematerial: The bar chart illustrates the mean annual damages in trillions of dollars in the U.S. due to climate-related issues for three different years: 2050, 2070, and 2090. The damages are categorized by sector, with each category distinguished by color... In 2050, the total damage is just over $1 trillion. By 2070, the damage rises to approximately $2 trillion. In 2090, the damages reach close to $3 trillion. Image: Approximate value from bar chart... Table 1. The 95% confidence interval (CI) and mean annual US climate-driven damages in 2090 for the six categories shown in Fig. 2. All values are in 2020 USD. Totals may not sum due to rounding... TotalinFrEDI USD2900 USD510–USD12000... hours of work when temperatures are too hot for workers to work outdoors or in unconditioned workplaces (e.g., warehouses). Table 2 provides the mean physical impacts from each of the sectors in the health and labor categories in 2090, along with the 95% CI. As shown in Table 2, climate-driven changes in temperature have the largest impact on premature mortality, resulting in nearly 50000 additional deaths (95% CI: 19000–91000 deaths) annually by 2090, followed by climate-driven changes in air quality (5100 deaths; 95% CI: 2100–10000 deaths) and exposure to wildfire smoke (1100 deaths; 95% CI: 460–1700 deaths)... Category Mean(billions) 95%CI(billions)... Health USD2600 USD350–USD11000... To further illustrate the distribution of monetized damages across sectors, Fig. 4 shows the range of 2090 annual climate-driven damages in each of the 20 sectors in FrEDI, across all 10000 RFF-SP emission, GDP, and population scenarios, in decreasing order of sectoral mean damages. Figure 4 shows that national total damages in 2090 are primarily driven by the valuation of premature mortality attributable to climate-driven changes in temperature (mean: USD2.3 trillion per year; 95% CI: USD0.31–USD9.9 trillion per year). The next four sectors with the largest monetary climate-driven damages include premature mortality attributable to changes in air quality (mean: USD240 billion per year; 95% CI: USD32–USD1000 billion per year), transportation impacts associated with changes in high-tide flooding (mean: USD140 billion per year; 95% CI: USD110–USD200 billion per year), national labor hours lost (mean: USD51 billion per year; 95% CI: USD6.7–USD210 billion per year), and health damages from wildfire smoke exposure and response costs from wildfire suppression (mean: USD51 billion per year; 95% CI: USD8.1–USD220 billion per year). Climate-driven damages to coastal properties associated with changes in tropical storm frequency and wind strength (mean: USD28 billion per year; 95% CI: USD12-USD49 billion per year), damages attributable to changes in rail (mean: USD19 billion per year; 95% CI: USD7.7–USD45 billion per year) and road systems (mean: USD17 billion per year; 95% CI: USD6.6–USD35 billion per year), health damages from changes in southwestern dust exposure (mean: USD18 billion per year ; 95% CI: USD2.5–USD77 billion per year), and the health burden of change in Valley fever incidence (mean: USD14 billion per year; 95% CI: USD2.0–USD58 billion per year) round out the top 10 sectors with the largest annual damages in 2090.

Table 2 provides the mean physical impacts from each of the sectors in the health and labor categories in 2090, along with the 95% CI. As shown in Table 2, climate-driven changes in temperature have the largest impact on premature mortality, resulting in nearly 50000 additional deaths (95% CI: 19000–91000 deaths) annually by 2090, followed by climate-driven changes in air quality (5100 deaths; 95% CI: 2100–10000 deaths) and exposure to wildfire smoke (1100 deaths; 95% CI: 460–1700 deaths)... To further illustrate the distribution of monetized damages across sectors, Fig. 4 shows the range of 2090 annual climate-driven damages in each of the 20 sectors in FrEDI, across all 10000 RFF-SP emission, GDP, and population scenarios, in decreasing order of sectoral mean damages. sourcematerial: The bar chart illustrates the mean annual damages in trillions of dollars in the U.S. due to climate-related issues for three different years: 2050, 2070, and 2090. The damages are categorized by sector, with each category distinguished by color... In 2050, the total damage is just over $1 trillion. By 2070, the damage rises to approximately $2 trillion. In 2090, the damages reach close to $3 trillion. Image: Approximate value from bar chart... Table 1. The 95% confidence interval (CI) and mean annual US climate-driven damages in 2090 for the six categories shown in Fig. 2. All values are in 2020 USD. Totals may not sum due to rounding... TotalinFrEDI USD2900 USD510–USD12000... hours of work when temperatures are too hot for workers to work outdoors or in unconditioned workplaces (e.g., warehouses). Table 2 provides the mean physical impacts from each of the sectors in the health and labor categories in 2090, along with the 95% CI. As shown in Table 2, climate-driven changes in temperature have the largest impact on premature mortality, resulting in nearly 50000 additional deaths (95% CI: 19000–91000 deaths) annually by 2090, followed by climate-driven changes in air quality (5100 deaths; 95% CI: 2100–10000 deaths) and exposure to wildfire smoke (1100 deaths; 95% CI: 460–1700 deaths)... Category Mean(billions) 95%CI(billions)... Health USD2600 USD350–USD11000... To further illustrate the distribution of monetized damages across sectors, Fig. 4 shows the range of 2090 annual climate-driven damages in each of the 20 sectors in FrEDI, across all 10000 RFF-SP emission, GDP, and population scenarios, in decreasing order of sectoral mean damages. Figure 4 shows that national total damages in 2090 are primarily driven by the valuation of premature mortality attributable to climate-driven changes in temperature (mean: USD2.3 trillion per year; 95% CI: USD0.31–USD9.9 trillion per year). The next four sectors with the largest monetary climate-driven damages include premature mortality attributable to changes in air quality (mean: USD240 billion per year; 95% CI: USD32–USD1000 billion per year), transportation impacts associated with changes in high-tide flooding (mean: USD140 billion per year; 95% CI: USD110–USD200 billion per year), national labor hours lost (mean: USD51 billion per year; 95% CI: USD6.7–USD210 billion per year), and health damages from wildfire smoke exposure and response costs from wildfire suppression (mean: USD51 billion per year; 95% CI: USD8.1–USD220 billion per year). Climate-driven damages to coastal properties associated with changes in tropical storm frequency and wind strength (mean: USD28 billion per year; 95% CI: USD12-USD49 billion per year), damages attributable to changes in rail (mean: USD19 billion per year; 95% CI: USD7.7–USD45 billion per year) and road systems (mean: USD17 billion per year; 95% CI: USD6.6–USD35 billion per year), health damages from changes in southwestern dust exposure (mean: USD18 billion per year ; 95% CI: USD2.5–USD77 billion per year), and the health burden of change in Valley fever incidence (mean: USD14 billion per year; 95% CI: USD2.0–USD58 billion per year) round out the top 10 sectors with the largest annual damages in 2090.

More Notes: Mean and 95% CI: health damages from changes in southwestern dust exposure.

sourcematerial: The study utilizes the reduced-complexity model, the Framework for Evaluating Damages and Impacts (FrEDI), to project impacts across 10000 future scenarios through 2300. The analysis is performed using the FrEDI model, which calculates climate-driven damages by integrating randomly sampled scenarios of emissions, population, and GDP over time from the RFF-SPs. FrEDI computes differential impacts based on demographics at the census tract level, using current demographic data and projected climate-driven damages. The analysis projects damages based on temperature/sea level rise, damage functions, and sector-specific scalars, resulting in annual estimates. Results from FrEDI show that net national damages increase overtime, with mean climate-driven damages estimated to reach USD 2.9 trillion (95 % confidence interval (CI): USD 510 billion to USD 12 trillion) annually by 2090. Detailed FrEDI results show that the majority of annual long-term (e.g., 2090) damages are associated with climate change impacts to human health, including mortality attributable to climate-driven changes in temperature and air pollution (O3 and PM2.5) exposure. Regional results also show that annual long-term climate-driven damages vary geographically. The Southeast (all regions are as defined in Fig. 5) is projected to experience the largest annual damages per capita (mean: USD 9300 per person annually; 95 % CI: USD 1800-USD 37 000 per person annually), whereas the smallest damages per capita are expected in the Southwest (mean: USD 6300 per person annually; 95 % CI: USD 840-USD 27 000 per person annually).; Sectoral damages exhibit significant sensitivity to assumptions in adaptation scenarios, highlighting uncertainty in projections.; Various studies and approaches discussed, with emphasis on evaluating climate change impacts."Analysis projects damages for the year 2090, considering different adaptation scenarios (e.g., no additional adaptation, reactive adaptation, reasonably anticipated adaptation, proactive adaptation) and one precursor emissions scenario." The study utilizes the reduced-complexity model, the Framework for Evaluating Damages and Impacts (FrEDI), to project impacts across 10000 future scenarios through 2300. The analysis is performed using the FrEDI model, which calculates climate-driven damages by integrating randomly sampled scenarios of emissions, population, and GDP over time from the RFF-SPs. FrEDI computes differential impacts based on demographics at the census tract level, using current demographic data and projected climate-driven damages. The analysis projects damages based on temperature/sea level rise, damage functions, and sector-specific scalars, resulting in annual estimates. Results from FrEDI show that net national damages increase overtime, with mean climate-driven damages estimated to reach USD 2.9 trillion (95 % confidence interval (CI): USD 510 billion to USD 12 trillion) annually by 2090. Detailed FrEDI results show that the majority of annual long-term (e.g., 2090) damages are associated with climate change impacts to human health, including mortality attributable to climate-driven changes in temperature and air pollution (O3 and PM2.5) exposure. Regional results also show that annual long-term climate-driven damages vary geographically. The Southeast (all regions are as defined in Fig. 5) is projected to experience the largest annual damages per capita (mean: USD 9300 per person annually; 95 % CI: USD 1800-USD 37 000 per person annually), whereas the smallest damages per capita are expected in the Southwest (mean: USD 6300 per person annually; 95 % CI: USD 840-USD 27 000 per person annually).; Sectoral damages exhibit significant sensitivity to assumptions in adaptation scenarios, highlighting uncertainty in projections.; Various studies and approaches discussed, with emphasis on evaluating climate change impacts."Analysis projects damages for the year 2090, considering different adaptation scenarios (e.g., no additional adaptation, reactive adaptation, reasonably anticipated adaptation, proactive adaptation) and one precursor emissions scenario." Table A3 presents annual mean (and 95% confidence interval) climate-driven damages in 2090 for sectors that include different adaptation options. The damages are reported in billions of dollars (2020 USD). For example, electricity transmission and distribution has damages of USD 12 billion per year without adaptation (95% CI: USD 7.3 billion - USD 18 billion) and decreases to USD 6.3 billion per year with proactive adaptation (95% CI: USD 4.9 billion - USD 8.3 billion). Rail sector damages also decrease significantly from USD 21 billion per year without adaptation (95% CI: USD 7.2 billion - USD 55 billion) to USD 1.5 billion per year with proactive adaptation (95% CI: USD 0.28 billion - USD 3.9 billion). Roads sector damages reduce from USD 130 billion per year without adaptation (95% CI: USD 25 billion - USD 330 billion) to USD 7.3 billion per year with proactive adaptation (95% CI: USD 5.8 billion - USD 8.4 billion). Coastal properties show some reduction in damages from USD 16 billion per year without adaptation (95% CI: USD 9.9 billion - USD 37 billion) to USD 7.5 billion per year with proactive adaptation (95% CI: USD 7.0 billion - USD 8.3 billion). Transportation impacts from high-tide flooding are documented at USD 890 billion per year without adaptation (95% CI: USD 680 billion - USD 1200 billion) but reduce significantly with reasonably anticipated adaptation to USD 140 billion per year (95% CI: USD 110 billion - USD 200 billion) and direct adaptation further reduces it to USD 1.9 billion per year (95% CI: USD 1.3 billion - USD 3.4 billion).
